g.data-narration: Welcome to Multiple Stacks and Queues - exploring advanced data structure implementations.
Real applications need multiple data structures - like handling nested function calls and task scheduling.
Two stacks share one array by growing from opposite ends - Stack 1 from left, Stack 2 from right.
Key data members include the array, top1 pointer at -1, and top2 pointer at n for Stack 2.
The overflow condition is when top1 + 1 equals top2 - the two stack tops would cross.
Let's walk through pushing 10,20 to Stack1 and 30,40 to Stack2 - watch the array fill from both ends.
Fixed division for multiple stacks wastes space - one stack overflows while others have room.
Circular queue wraps around using modulo arithmetic, solving the space wastage problem of linear queues.
Remember: Enqueue uses rear = (rear + 1) % MAX_SIZE, and Dequeue uses front = (front + 1) % MAX_SIZE.
A circular queue of size n can hold max n-1 elements to distinguish full from empty conditions.
g.text: Wastes 1 space to distinguish from empty

g.data-narration: Watch how elements wrap around - after enqueueing 50 at index 4, the next rear goes back to index 0.
Deque allows insertion and deletion at both ends - combining stack and queue properties.
Two deque types: Input-restricted allows deletion from both ends but insertion at one end only.
Priority queue serves elements by priority, not FIFO - smallest element has highest priority in ascending type.
Array-based priority queue has O(n) deletion complexity, while heap-based provides efficient O(log n) operations.
Key exam points: Two stacks overflow at top1+1==top2, circular queue wastes 1 space, heap gives O(log n) priority queue.
g.text: ✓ Circular queue: array size = max elements + 1
✓ Priority queue: heap = O(log n), array = O(n)
